steps.title: Identify the grammatical role of 'falling' in the sentence
steps.content: The word “falling” ends the subordinate clause “while falling” which is embedded inside the larger subject phrase: “the reason seeds from a dying dandelion appear to float in the air while falling.” Nothing in this clause requires punctuation after “falling”—it is in the middle of an uninterrupted noun phrase, not at the end of a supplemental element.

steps.title: Eliminate punctuated choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — falling,: A comma here would incorrectly split the subject (“the reason seeds … while falling”) from its main verb (“is”). ✗
  • Choice B — falling:: A colon here is grammatically impossible; the material before it is not an independent clause. ✗
  • Choice C — falling;: A semicolon joins two independent clauses, but “the reason seeds … while falling” is a subject phrase, not an independent clause. ✗
  • Choice D — falling: No punctuation allows the subject to connect directly to its main verb “is”—the only grammatically correct option. ✓
D is correct.
